header: S. S. da Grama (SP) / Pocos de Caldas (MG)

lesson.menu_desc: Asking Deep Questions -- Question forms + Past simple -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 7 -- Culture Curious -- Asking Deep Questions
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Tuca Dias | Aula 7 -- Culture Curious

listenings.text: Hi everyone, my name is Kenji and I come from Kyoto, in Japan. I grew up in a small neighborhood near a famous old temple, so traditions were always a big part of my life. When I was young, my grandmother taught me the etiquette of the tea ceremony, and we celebrated many cultural festivals together every year. Five years ago I moved abroad to study, and at first I felt a little homesick and out of place. People asked me a lot of curious questions about my heritage, my customs, and my daily lifestyle. Some of those questions led to small misunderstandings, but most of them helped me make good friends. I learned that when someone is genuinely curious and asks respectful questions about your background, it is a wonderful way to connect. So please, do not be shy. Be curious, ask good questions, and listen carefully to the answer.

Hey, it is Tuca again. I just finished my culture interview with Kenji, the exchange student from Japan, and it went really well. I prepared ten questions before we met, and I am so glad I did. I asked him where he grew up, what traditions his family kept, and how he learned to live abroad. He told me about the tea ceremony, the festivals from his childhood, and the etiquette he had to teach his new friends. I was a little nervous about the question order at first, but I remembered the rule: question word, then did, then the subject, then the base verb. Where did you grow up? What did your family celebrate? It worked perfectly. Honestly, I learned so much about a culture that was completely new to me, and Kenji said he loved sharing his heritage. Next time I want to interview someone from a different country again.

extra_audio.text: Tuca wanted to learn about a different culture, so she met Kenji, an exchange student from Japan. First, she prepared ten questions about his background and traditions. Then she asked where he grew up and what his family celebrated. Kenji described the tea ceremony and the festivals from his childhood. They cleared up one small misunderstanding about a custom. Finally, Tuca thanked him for sharing his heritage and culture.
